items.prompt: This 1803 decision by Chief Justice John Marshall established the principle of judicial review — the Supreme Court's power to strike down laws that violate the Constitution. Which case?
items.explanation:
Decided 1803; Chief Justice John Marshall wrote the unanimous opinion
William Marbury sued Secretary of State James Madison for his undelivered appointment as justice of the peace
Marshall ruled against Marbury on the merits but used the case to assert judicial review — the power to void unconstitutional laws
The Constitution does not explicitly grant judicial review; Marshall inferred it from Article III
Foundation of the U.S. system of checks and balances — establishes courts as a co-equal branch
Considered Marshall's greatest opinion and one of the most consequential in U.S. history

items.prompt: This 1819 decision upheld Congress's power to create a national bank and ruled that states cannot tax federal institutions, establishing the broad reach of the Necessary and Proper Clause. Which case?
items.explanation:
Decided 1819; Chief Justice John Marshall wrote the unanimous opinion
Maryland attempted to tax the Second Bank of the United States; cashier James McCulloch refused to pay
Marshall held two principles: (1) Congress has implied powers under the Necessary and Proper Clause; (2) states cannot tax federal institutions
'The power to tax involves the power to destroy' — famous Marshall quote
Broadly expanded federal power; central to federalism debates ever since
items.cardFront: Federal implied powers and Necessary & Proper Clause (1819)

links.text: Federal implied powers and Necessary & Proper Clause

items.prompt: This 1857 decision ruled that African Americans were not citizens, that Congress had no power to ban slavery in the territories, and declared the Missouri Compromise unconstitutional. Which case?
items.explanation:
Decided March 6, 1857; Chief Justice Roger Taney wrote the majority opinion
Dred Scott was an enslaved man who sued for freedom after living in free territories with his owner
Court ruled 7-2: Black people (free or enslaved) were not citizens and had no right to sue in federal court
Declared the Missouri Compromise unconstitutional — Congress could not ban slavery in territories
Widely considered the worst Supreme Court decision in history; dramatically inflamed sectional tensions
Effectively overruled by the 13th Amendment (1865) and 14th Amendment (1868)
items.cardFront: African Americans not citizens; slavery in territories (1857)

links.text: African Americans not citizens; slavery in territories

items.prompt: This 1896 decision established the 'separate but equal' doctrine, upholding Louisiana's law requiring separate railroad cars for Black and white passengers. Which case?
items.explanation:
Decided May 18, 1896; 7-1 ruling (Justice John Marshall Harlan dissented)
Homer Plessy was 7/8 white but classified as 'colored' under Louisiana law; he purposely tested the law
Court upheld 'separate but equal' — segregation does not violate the Equal Protection Clause if facilities are nominally equal
Justice Harlan's lone dissent: 'Our constitution is color-blind'
Remained dominant legal doctrine for 58 years — provided constitutional cover for Jim Crow segregation throughout the South
Overruled by Brown v. Board of Education (1954)

items.prompt: This 1919 case upheld the conviction of a socialist who distributed anti-draft pamphlets during World War I, with Justice Holmes introducing the 'clear and present danger' test for free speech limits. Which case?
items.explanation:
Decided 1919; Justice Oliver Wendell Holmes wrote the unanimous opinion
Charles Schenck convicted under the Espionage Act for distributing pamphlets opposing the WWI draft
Holmes articulated the 'clear and present danger' test: speech may be restricted when it poses an imminent danger
'Falsely shouting fire in a crowded theatre' analogy comes from Holmes' opinion
Schenck standard was later refined and superseded by Brandenburg v. Ohio (1969), which requires imminent lawless action
Key moment in First Amendment jurisprudence — first major free speech ruling

items.prompt: This 1954 landmark decision unanimously overturned 'separate but equal', ruling that racially segregated public schools were inherently unequal and violated the Equal Protection Clause of the 14th Amendment. Which case?
items.explanation:
Decided May 17, 1954; unanimous ruling by Chief Justice Earl Warren
Consolidated five cases from Kansas, South Carolina, Virginia, Delaware, and Washington D.C.
Thurgood Marshall argued for the NAACP; later became the first Black Supreme Court Justice (1967)
Warren: 'Separate educational facilities are inherently unequal'
Eisenhower sent federal troops to Little Rock Central High School to enforce desegregation (1957)
Considered the most important civil rights legal victory of the 20th century

items.prompt: This 1963 decision ruled that states must provide an attorney to criminal defendants who cannot afford one, establishing the public defender system nationwide. Which case?
items.explanation:
Decided March 18, 1963; unanimous ruling by Justice Hugo Black
Clarence Earl Gideon, accused of burglary in Florida, was denied a court-appointed attorney and convicted
Gideon wrote his own handwritten petition to the Supreme Court from prison
Held: the 6th Amendment right to counsel applies to states via 14th Amendment incorporation
Forced all 50 states to provide public defenders — created the modern public defender system
After retrial with an attorney, Gideon was acquitted
items.cardFront: Right to counsel for defendants who can't afford one (1963)

links.text: Right to counsel for defendants who can't afford one

items.prompt: This 1966 decision required police to inform suspects of their rights before interrogation, creating the now-familiar warnings about remaining silent and having an attorney. Which case?
items.explanation:
Decided June 13, 1966; 5-4 ruling by Chief Justice Warren
Ernesto Miranda confessed to rape and kidnapping after police interrogation without being told his rights
Requires police to advise suspects: right to remain silent; anything said can be used against them; right to an attorney
Miranda warnings now standard procedure in all U.S. jurisdictions
Miranda himself was retried without the confession, convicted again, and stabbed to death in 1976
Rooted in 5th Amendment (self-incrimination) and 6th Amendment (right to counsel)
items.cardFront: Police must advise suspects of rights before interrogation (1966)

links.text: Police must advise suspects of rights before interrogation

items.prompt: This 1967 unanimous decision struck down Virginia's anti-miscegenation law, ruling that laws banning interracial marriage violated the Equal Protection and Due Process Clauses. Which case?
items.explanation:
Decided June 12, 1967; unanimous ruling by Chief Justice Warren
Richard Loving (white) and Mildred Loving (Black and Native American) were convicted in Virginia for their marriage
In a single ruling, struck down anti-miscegenation laws in 16 states
Established the constitutional right to marry the person of one's choice — later cited in Obergefell
June 12 is now celebrated as 'Loving Day' by interracial couples nationwide
The name 'Loving' was noted by Warren as 'an ideal name for this decision'

items.prompt: This 1969 decision held that public school students do not 'shed their constitutional rights at the schoolhouse gate', protecting students' right to wear black armbands protesting the Vietnam War. Which case?
items.explanation:
Decided February 24, 1969; 7-2 ruling by Justice Abe Fortas
Mary Beth Tinker (age 13), her brother John, and Christopher Eckhardt wore black armbands to protest Vietnam War; all were suspended
'Students do not shed their constitutional rights at the schoolhouse gate'
Test: student speech is protected unless it 'materially and substantially disrupts' school operations
Landmark protection of student free speech rights; frequently cited in school free speech cases
items.cardFront: Student free speech in public schools (1969)

links.text: Student free speech in public schools

items.prompt: This 1973 decision established a constitutional right to abortion based on a right to privacy in the 14th Amendment. It was overturned 49 years later by Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization (2022). Which case?
items.explanation:
Decided January 22, 1973; 7-2 ruling by Justice Harry Blackmun
'Jane Roe' was Norma McCorvey; Henry Wade was the Dallas district attorney who enforced the Texas abortion ban
Recognized abortion as a constitutional right via an implied 'right to privacy' in the 14th Amendment's due process clause
Established a trimester framework: states could regulate abortion after the first trimester
Constitutional protection stood for 49 years before being overturned by Dobbs v. Jackson (June 24, 2022)
Dobbs returned abortion regulation to individual states — the most consequential reversal in Supreme Court history

items.prompt: This unanimous 1974 decision ruled that President Nixon had to turn over the White House tape recordings, rejecting his claim of absolute executive privilege and leading directly to his resignation. Which case?
items.explanation:
Decided July 24, 1974; unanimous 8-0 (Nixon appointee Rehnquist recused himself)
Nixon claimed absolute executive privilege to withhold Watergate tape recordings from the special prosecutor
Court held: executive privilege exists but is not absolute — it must yield to due process in criminal proceedings
18½-minute gap discovered in the 'smoking gun' tape after Nixon was ordered to hand recordings over
Nixon resigned August 9, 1974 — sixteen days after the ruling
Established the principle that no person, including the president, is above the law
items.cardFront: No absolute executive privilege — Watergate tapes (1974)

links.text: No absolute executive privilege — Watergate tapes

items.prompt: This 2000 decision stopped the Florida recount in the presidential election on Equal Protection grounds, effectively deciding the election in favor of George W. Bush over Al Gore. Which case?
items.explanation:
Decided December 12, 2000; 5-4 to stop the recount
Florida's recount was ordered after an extremely close count — Bush was ultimately certified the winner by 537 votes
Court ruled: the varying recount standards across Florida counties violated Equal Protection
Per curiam opinion unusually noted it should not be cited as precedent — limiting its value as a legal precedent
Gore won the national popular vote by ~540,000; Bush won Electoral College 271-266
Five Republican-appointed justices provided the majority; considered one of the most controversial SCOTUS decisions
items.cardFront: Florida recount stopped — 2000 presidential election decided (2000)

links.text: Florida recount stopped — 2000 presidential election decided

items.prompt: This 2015 decision held that same-sex couples have a fundamental constitutional right to marry, invalidating same-sex marriage bans in all 50 states. Which case?
items.explanation:
Decided June 26, 2015; 5-4 decision written by Justice Anthony Kennedy
Jim Obergefell sued Ohio for refusing to list him as surviving spouse on his partner's death certificate
Held: the 14th Amendment requires states to license and recognize same-sex marriages
Kennedy's opinion invoked Loving v. Virginia and the right to dignity and equal recognition
Kennedy: 'No union is more profound than marriage'
Came on the same date as U.S. v. Windsor (2013, struck down DOMA) — June 26 became significant in LGBTQ+ rights history
items.cardFront: Same-sex marriage is a constitutional right (2015)

links.text: Same-sex marriage is a constitutional right

items.prompt: This 2022 decision overturned Roe v. Wade and Planned Parenthood v. Casey, ruling that the Constitution does not protect a right to abortion and returning the issue to individual states. Which case?
items.answer: Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization
alternates: Dobbs v Jackson Women's Health Organization
items.explanation:
Decided June 24, 2022; 6-3 majority opinion written by Justice Samuel Alito
Challenged Mississippi's 15-week abortion ban; Thomas Dobbs was the Mississippi state health officer
Overturned both Roe v. Wade (1973) and Planned Parenthood v. Casey (1992)
Held: the Constitution makes no reference to abortion; the right is not deeply rooted in history
Draft opinion leaked in May 2022 — an unprecedented breach of Supreme Court confidentiality
As of 2025, 21 states have enacted total or near-total abortion bans following the ruling
items.cardFront: Roe overturned — abortion returned to states (2022)
items.cardBack: Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization

links.text: Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization
Roe overturned — abortion returned to states
